checklist.entry: Treat resources as server-exposed context or data identified by URI.
Validate resource URIs and enforce access controls for sensitive resources.
Record MIME type, provenance, freshness, sensitivity and intended audience.
Do not expose research evidence or user data through resources without permission, minimisation and audit controls.
Treat prompts as user-controlled reusable templates or workflows exposed by servers.
Validate prompt inputs and outputs to reduce injection and unauthorized-resource risks.
Document prompt arguments, embedded resources and expected user initiation path.
Treat tools as model-invokable functions that can execute code paths or external operations.
Require explicit user consent before invoking tools with side effects or sensitive access.
Validate tool arguments before execution and validate tool results before feeding them into further automated action.
Treat tool descriptions and annotations as untrusted unless obtained from a trusted server and reviewed.
Record tool name, schema, side effects, idempotency, retry behaviour and failure handling.
Classify every tool invocation into tier-1 (auto-allow read-only), tier-2 (explicit invocation consent required) or tier-3 (elevated approval required).
Tier-1 examples: repository listing, file reads, schema introspection and other low-risk read-only inspection paths.
Tier-2 examples: file writes, commits, pull-request changes, external API mutations and chargeable model operations.
Tier-3 examples: permission model changes, secrets operations, destructive production actions or broad data export.
When blocked, return policy statuses and remediation steps rather than claiming tools are disabled.
